incorrects.description: Student selected 'Counting items one by one', which does not benefit from scale of 10

params.description: Individual counting image highlights to show why this doesn't need scale of 10.

steps.text: Let's think about this together. Counting items one by one works fine without grouping by tens. Where would grouping by tens actually help?

incorrects.description: Student selected at least one wrong option or missed a correct option on first attempt

params.description: Money image pulses to draw attention to grouping by tens.
Ruler image pulses to draw attention to marks in tens.

steps.text: Here's a hint: think about situations where you naturally group things by tens or skip count by tens.

incorrects.description: Student selected at least one wrong option or missed a correct option on second attempt

params.description: Money image highlights showing stacks organized by tens.
Ruler image highlights showing measurement marks in tens.

steps.text: You're working on it. Money comes in stacks of tens. Rulers have marks at 10, 20, 30. Both use scale of 10 because the numbers are bigger.

incorrects.description: Student answered incorrectly three or more times. System models the answer.

params.description: Money image highlights as system demonstrates correct answer.
Ruler image highlights as system demonstrates correct answer.

steps.text: Let me show you how this works. Money and measuring both use tens because they deal with bigger numbers. You count money in stacks of 10 dollar bills. You measure with a ruler marked at 10, 20, 30. Counting items one by one doesn't need grouping by tens. Select money and ruler.
